text: Keystone habits, as Duhigg describes them, are habits whose adoption triggers cascading changes in other areas of behavior. Exercise is a canonical example: people who begin a regular workout routine report eating better, sleeping more consistently, and feeling more productive at work — none of which were explicitly targeted. The mechanism appears to be the internalization of a new self-concept: 'I am someone who takes care of myself.' This identity shift then acts as a meta-cue that reorients dozens of smaller decisions. Paul O'Neill's transformation of Alcoa by targeting worker safety habits is Duhigg's most dramatic organizational example of this cascading effect.
question: According to the passage, what mechanism explains why keystone habits trigger changes in unrelated behaviors?

options.text: Keystone habits directly rewire the basal ganglia for multiple routines simultaneously
The dopamine system generalizes rewards from one habit to adjacent behaviors
Adopting a new identity or self-concept acts as a meta-cue reorienting many smaller decisions
Cognitive load freed by one habit provides mental resources for forming others
